===== Beck2009_DevDyn_Xenopus+Regeneration =====
SPECIAL ISSUE REVIEWS–A PEER REVIEWED FORUM
Beyond Early Development: Xenopus as an
Emerging Model for the Study of Regenerative
Mechanisms
Caroline W. Beck,1* Juan Carlos Izpisu´ a Belmonte,2,3 and Bea Christen2
While Xenopus is a well-known model system for early vertebrate development, in recent years, it has also
emerged as a leading model for regeneration research. As an anuran amphibian, Xenopus laevis can
regenerate the larval tail and limb by means of the formation of a proliferating blastema, the lens of the eye
by transdifferentiation of nearby tissues, and also exhibits a partial regeneration of the postmetamorphic
froglet forelimb. With the availability of inducible transgenic techniques for Xenopus, recent experiments
are beginning to address the functional role of genes in the process of regeneration. The use of soluble
inhibitors has also been very successful in this model. Using the more traditional advantages of Xenopus,
others are providing important lineage data on the origin of the cells that make up the tissues of the
regenerate. Finally, transcriptome analyses of regenerating tissues seek to identify the genes and cellular
processes that enable successful regeneration. Developmental Dynamics 238:1226–1248, 2009.
© 2009 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Key words: Xenopus; regeneration; blastema; epimorphic; transdifferentiation; tail; limb; lens; transcriptome; Hsp70
Accepted 6 January 2009
INTRODUCTION
While all animals have evolved strat-
egies to respond to injury and disease,
a minority are also able to replace lost
appendages or organ parts. This type
of regeneration occurs rarely and in a
limited way among the well-charac-
terized vertebrate model organisms.
Mice can regenerate their extreme
digit tips and zebraﬁsh their ﬁns,
brain, and heart tissue. Urodele am-
phibians (those that retain the tail af-
ter metamorphosis such as the newts
and salamanders as well as the neo-
tenic axolotl) are much better at re-
generation and can restore limbs,
tails, the lens1 and retina of the eye,
and heart tissue, even as adults. This
remarkable ability makes urodeles a
very attractive model for regeneration
study, and much progress has been
made in recent years in unlocking the
secrets of the urodeles’ regenerative
powers, which seem to depend, at
least in part, on their ability to de-
differentiate adult cells and return
them to a proliferating, embryonic-
like state (for review, see Brockes and
Kumar, 2002; Chaar and Tsilﬁdis,
2006; Straube and Tanaka, 2006).
Here, we review classical and con-
temporary evidence that the aquatic
South African clawed frog Xenopus
laevis, which has contributed so much
to our understanding of vertebrate de-
velopment over the past 20 years, is
also an ideal model to study regener-
ation. The embryos develop ex utero
and have a free-living larval stage,
which makes them accessible for ma-
nipulation at all stages of develop-
ment. Xenopus are easy to keep and
can be spawned all year round in re-
sponse to a simple hormone injection,
1With the exception of the axolotl, which cannot
regenerate a lost lens.
1Department of Zoology and Genetics Otago, University of Otago, New Zealand
2Center of Regenerative Medicine in Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain
3Gene Expression Laboratory, Salk Institute for Biological Studies, La Jolla, California
*Correspondence to: Caroline W. Beck, Department of Zoology, University of Otago, P.O. Box 56, Dunedin 9064, New Zealand.
E-mail: caroline.beck@otago.ac.nz
DOI 10.1002/dvdy.21890
Published online 11 March 2009 in Wiley InterScience (www.interscience.wiley.com).
DEVELOPMENTAL DYNAMICS 238:1226–1248, 2009
© 2009 Wiley-Liss, Inc.

producing thousands of offspring at a
time, and both normal development
and regeneration are well described.
Xenopus and other frogs are anuran
amphibians, that is, they lose their
tails at metamorphosis. Like most
urodeles, they also possess the ability
to regenerate tails, limbs, and the lens
of the eye, but unlike the urodeles,
this regenerative potential is limited.
The
regenerated
tadpole
tail
is
slightly different from the original,
and does not regenerate at all over a
brief developmental period, and the
regenerative capacity of the limbs and
lens undergoes an ontogenic decline,
whereby younger animals regenerate
better than older ones. Far from being
a disadvantage, this stage-dependent
regenerative ability means that Xeno-
pus provides a system in which the
progressive loss of regenerative ability
can be studied. This enables a unique
approach in which both functional loss
and gain of function experiments di-
rected at understanding the molecular
processes controlling regeneration can
be used. In addition, as a well-estab-
lished model organism for the study of
embryonic development, useful tools
are available for this system, such as
grafting protocols, transgenic tech-
niques, extensive collections of ex-
pressed sequence tags (ESTs), and
whole-mount in situ hybridization.
This combination of genetic manipula-
tion, fecundity, and variable regener-
ative ability make the clawed frog an
excellent model system in which to
study the molecular basis of regener-
ative phenomena.
In the ﬁrst section, we review the
process of regeneration in Xenopus.
We will outline and illustrate the mor-
phological stages of each process and
discuss the limits to regenerative abil-
ity. Next, we will summarize and syn-
thesize the results of recent work that
use candidate gene approaches or
gene-hunting strategies to identify
the molecular mechanisms that drive
and control the regenerative process.
Strategies for activating (gain of func-
tion) and inhibiting (loss of function)
biochemical processes in Xenopus lar-
vae have been developed and com-
bined, and a molecular pathway for
regeneration of the tail is beginning to
fall into place. Candidate gene ap-
proaches
are
ideal
for
identifying
mechanisms common to development
or in homeostasis, but it is likely that
novel mechanisms also play a part in
regeneration in Xenopus. We will dis-
cuss
gene-hunting
strategies
that
have been used with a view to identi-
fying genes whose expression appears
to be differentially regulated during
the process of regeneration. There
have been several such studies, and
partial meta-analysis of some of these
data suggests that several genes are
commonly associated with regenera-
tion. In the ﬁnal, perspectives section,
we discuss our vision for the future of
the ﬁeld and describe some exciting
new leads that may establish Xenopus
even more ﬁrmly as a leading regen-
eration model over the next few years.
It would be remiss to write such a
section without mentioning the closely
related Xenopus (Silurana) tropicalis,
which is now the preferred amphibian
species for genetic analyses. With its
true diploid status and shorter gener-
ation time, known genome sequence
and possibilities for true genetic ma-
nipulation, this species opens up new
and exciting possibilities for regener-
ation research. We hope that this re-
view will both enable and encourage
more researchers to take up the chal-
lenge of understanding regenerative
processes in Xenopus.
1. REGENERATIVE
PROCESSES IN XENOPUS
In this ﬁrst section, we will describe
four of the better-known regenerative
processes that occur in Xenopus. Limb
bud, postmetamorphic limb, tail, and
lens regeneration will be discussed in
detail in this review. Due to space con-
straints, neither the regeneration of
the intestine during metamorphosis
nor the regeneration of the neural ret-
ina will be discussed here, and we di-
rect those interested in these pro-
cesses to recent publications by Slack
et al. (2008) and Yoshii et al. (2007).
We will deﬁne the limits of regenera-
tion in each case, and discuss which
processes are thought to inﬂuence
this. Progress toward understanding
the cell biology of the process will be
described speciﬁcally for Xenopus, as
there are some notable differences
from urodeles.
1.1 Tail Regeneration
The ability of Xenopus tadpoles to re-
generate the larval tail is intriguing,
because the tail is composed of the
same axial tissues (spinal cord, noto-
chord2, and somites3) as the trunk,
which cannot regenerate (Fig. 1). The
embryonic tail bud of Xenopus cannot
regenerate and removal of even a
small part of the tail bud results in a
deﬁcit in the ﬁnal tail (Tucker and
Slack, 1995b). In contrast, the larval
tail can regenerate (with the exception
noted below), from st. 404 right up un-
til metamorphic climax (st. 60) when
the tail is absorbed (Filoni et al., 1977;
Bosco, 1979). As much as 75% of the
post-anal tail can be safely removed
without
compromising
the
animal,
and a full-length tail is regenerated
after 10–14 days (Fig. 1A) regardless
of the anterior posterior level at which
the tail is cut. This is intriguing be-
cause the anterior anatomical or post-
anal tail is derived from trunk cells
and not from the tail bud, which con-
tributes just the posterior 40% of the
tail. The tail always regenerates in
the same way, regardless of the level
at which the tail is amputated; hence,
whether cells at the newly formed
stump are trunk or tail bud derived.
The morphology of this process has
been described in detail (Gargioli and
Slack, 2004). After amputation, epi-
dermal cells migrate to cover the
wound with a thin epithelial layer, the
wound
epithelium,
which
lacks
a
basement membrane. Over the next
few days, the cut end of the spinal cord
closes to form a blind-ended tube with
an enlarged distal lumen, the neural
ampulla (Fig. 1C; Stefanelli, 1951;
Filoni et al., 1977). The notochord,
which as a mature structure is com-
posed of large vacuolated cells sur-
2The notochord is an embryonic structure and is
not found in the postmetamorphic frog or the
retained tail of adult urodeles.
3The somites contribute to the vertebrae in the
trunk region, but in the post-anal tail, they de-
velop into muscles that degenerate at metamor-
phosis.
4Xenopus laevis development is divided into 65
anatomically identiﬁable stages according to
Nieuwkoop and Faber (1967). These stages are
referred to throughout this review as st., and
should not be confused with Freeman stages used
in lens regeneration.
XENOPUS REGENERATION 1227

rounded by a sheath of nonvacuolated
cells, develops a bullet-shaped mass of
cells at the cut end that may arise
from the peripheral sheath cells. Mus-
cle ﬁbers at the cut surface appear to
degenerate and undifferentiated, mes-
enchymal-like cells accumulate around
the neural ampulla and notochord tip
(Fig. 1C; Gargioli and Slack, 2004). It
is these mesenchymal-like cells that
most closely resemble a regeneration
blastema in the Xenopus tail (see Box
1).
An exception to the otherwise con-
tinuous regenerative ability of the tail
occurs at around st. 46–47, when the
tadpole begins feeding behavior. The
majority of tadpoles whose tails are
partially removed at this stage, called
the “refractory period,” do not regen-
erate any tail tissues, instead consis-
tently forming skin across the wound
(Beck et al., 2003). Regenerative ca-
pacity then increases from around
70% at st. 48 to close to 100% at st. 49
and above. The reason for the tran-
sient refractory period is not clear, but
wound healing is more rapid in tails
that are going to regenerate success-
fully, taking 6–12 hr, and the result-
ing wound epithelium is very thin. In
contrast, nonregenerating tail stumps
heal slower (24 hr) and become cov-
ered with a much more distinct, skin-
like epidermis. Interestingly, animals
operated at this stage can regenerate
tails if the tip of the stump is removed
after the refractory period is over, so it
seems that the skin-like epidermis
may be nonpermissive to regeneration
(Beck et al., 2003). Other differences
have been noted during the refractory
period, such as lower levels of endog-
enous
bone
morphogenetic
protein
(Bmp) transcripts (Beck et al., 2006), a
higher rate of apoptosis (Tseng et al.,
2007) and a failure of the wound epi-
thelial membrane to re-polarize nor-
mally (Adams et al., 2007). Identiﬁca-
tion of a refractory period in tail
regeneration has enabled researchers
to perform both gain and loss of func-
tion experiments in the same organ-
ism to identify regenerative mecha-
nisms (see Section 2).
The posterior portion of the post-
anal tail develops in part from the tail
bud, initially described as a simple
blastema (Deuchar, 1975a) and now
known to be composed of multiple dis-
crete expression zones established as
Fig. 1.
Regeneration of the tail. A: Time course of tail regeneration. Unoperated tail is shown at
the top (WT), labeled to show the arrangement of the axial tissues. Note that the spinal cord lies on
top of the notochord and is hard to see. After removal of the posterior 20% of the tail length, the
extent of regeneration is shown after 1, 4, 10, and 14 days. Note the continuity of the axial
structures between the original and regenerated tissues. B: Schematic of a transverse section
through a Xenopus tadpole tail to show the arrangement of the tissues that must be regenerated.
C: Sagittal section through a 3 day regenerating tadpole tail to show the spinal cord terminating in
the blind-ended neural ampulla. The neural ampulla typically contains an enlarged lumen, contin-
uous with the lumen of the stump spinal cord. The vacuolated notochord cells of the stump are
continuous with a bullet-shaped structure composed of cells resembling the sheath cells that
surround the notochord; later on, these cells will vacuolate and enlarge, becoming indistinguish-
able from the stump notochord cells. There are also undifferentiated, mesenchymal-like cells found
around these two structures. White arrows indicate amputation plane. Scale bar  200 m.
Box 1: Does the tail regenerate via a blastema or
regeneration bud?
The term blastema, when used to describe the signature epimorphic
regeneration of the urodele limb, is deﬁned as a mass of undifferentiated,
proliferating cells arising from epithelial-mesenchymal interactions and
containing intrinsic morphogenetic information (Carlson, 2007). Where
then, is the tail blastema? In addition to the neural ampulla and notochord,
there are a number of undifferentiated, mesenchymal-like cells found be-
neath the wound epithelium. Gargioli and Slack describe these cells as the
regeneration blastema, and combination of the blastema with the neural
ampulla and notochord cells as the regeneration bud (Gargioli and Slack,
2004). However, the rates of cell proliferation are highest in the spinal cord
and notochord, epithelium and ﬁns, with the lowest rate being found in the
undifferentiated mesenchyme (Beck et al., 2006). Perhaps the safest as-
sumption given current knowledge is that the neural ampulla, bullet like
notochord cells, and mesenchyme together make up the regeneration bud,
and that the term blastema does not really apply to the tail.
1228 BECK ET AL.

a result of interaction between three
key tissues in the posterior of the em-
bryo: the neural plate, posterior meso-
derm,
and
posterior
notochord
(Tucker and Slack, 1995a). In this
sense, the tail bud derived tail can be
thought of as developing rather like
the limb buds, as an outgrowth pro-
duced by the juxtaposition of cells
from different backgrounds. Develop-
ment of the tail bud is well studied
and
known
to
involve
signaling
through Bmp and Notch pathways
(Beck and Slack, 1998, 1999a, 2002;
Beck et al., 2001). During regenera-
tion, the process of replacing the lost
tail might be expected to use the same
mechanisms that were used in its de-
velopment. Several studies have com-
pared gene expression in developing
and regenerating tails. Both, genes
associated with Notch signalling and
Msx genes were expressed similarly
during development and regeneration
of the tail (Beck et al., 2003). Msx1
and 2 are transcriptional repressors
known
to
function
downstream
of
Bmp signalling (Suzuki et al., 1997)
and are associated with dedifferentia-
tion of muscle cells in urodele append-
age
regeneration
(Odelberg
et
al.,
2000). Other genes known to be in-
volved in development, such as poste-
rior Hox genes, are also re-expressed
during tail regeneration (Christen et
al., 2003), and ﬁbroblast growth fac-
tor-2 (fgf-2 or b-fgf) is up-regulated in
the
spinal
cord
after
amputation
(Zhang et al., 2000).
In addition to common mechanisms,
there are also reported differences be-
tween gene expression in developing
and regenerating tails. Two Bmp in-
hibitors, Chordin and Noggin1, are ex-
pressed in the developing tail bud, but
expression was not detected during re-
generation (Sugiura et al., 2004). It is
not known whether other mechanisms
exist in the regenerating tail to regu-
late Bmp signalling, but multiple nog-
gin homologues are known to exist in
Xenopus (Eroshkin et al., 2006). Nog-
gin-2 and -4 are not expressed in the
tail bud, but their expression during
regeneration has not been examined.
Furthermore, expression of the mor-
phogen sonic hedgehog (shh), nor-
mally found in the ﬂoor plate of the
ventral neural tube, appears to be ab-
sent from this tissue in regenerates
(Sugiura et al., 2004), suggesting that
spinal cord patterning of regenerates
may be defective.
Tail regeneration in Xenopus laevis
has been subject to intensive fate
mapping studies, which have yet to be
repeated in the limb or lens. Gargioli
and Slack (Gargioli and Slack, 2004)
used transgenic techniques developed
by Kroll and Amaya (1996) to create
embryos expressing green ﬂuorescent
protein (GFP) at high levels in the
majority of cells, using the viral cyto-
megalovirus (CMV) promoter. Regions
of tissue from CMV-GFP neurula em-
bryos corresponding to the future tail
muscle, notochord or spinal cord were
transplanted
into
wild-type
hosts.
Tadpoles that developed from these
animals were then subjected to partial
tail amputations, cutting through the
labeled region so that stump cells of
each particular axial tissue in turn
could be tracked in the regenerate. Re-
sults showed clearly that the spinal
cord regenerates exclusively from the
stump spinal cord (neural ampulla)
and the notochord from the notochord
stump cells, and that labeled stump
cells do not switch fate, because la-
beled cells are never found in other
tissues of the regenerate (Gargioli and
Slack, 2004; see Fig. 2A,B). The story
for muscle tissue though, was not so
straightforward.
In
tadpoles
with
muscle (presomitic mesoderm) labeled
grafts, no labeled cells were seen in
the regenerate (Fig. 2C). However,
when the same presomitic mesoderm
grafts were done using older embryos,
in which the neural tube has begun to
close and convergence and extension
of the mesoderm has begun, labeled
cells were seen in the regenerate (Fig.
2D).
Further
investigation
showed
that regenerated tail muscle does not
originate from the myoﬁbers, but from
a population of stem like satellite cells
that express pax7. These cells are
present in the later stage grafts but
not in the early stage grafts and are
necessary for muscle regeneration in
the Xenopus tadpole tail (Gargioli and
Slack, 2004; Chen et al., 2006b). While
the mechanisms of muscle regenera-
tion (by means of stem cell recruit-
ment) and that of the spinal cord and
notochord (hyperplasia) differ, an im-
portant observation from these results
is that there is no transdifferentiation
(a type of metaplasia, or switching of
cell fates) between the three axial tis-
sues. This lack of transdifferentiation
has also been conﬁrmed by a second
study utilizing transgenic tadpoles ex-
pressing yellow ﬂuorescent protein
(YFP) in the muscle cells. In this case,
the tissue speciﬁc promoter cardiac
actin was used, by means of a cre-lox
binary system, to show that no major
transdifferentiation of muscle cells to
other cell types occurs in the regener-
ate (Ryffel et al., 2003). This differs
markedly from the situation in urode-
les, for which dedifferentiating muscle
ﬁbers
contribute
to
the
blastema
(Echeverri et al., 2001), and transdif-
ferentiation
has
been
observed
(Echeverri and Tanaka, 2002). When
the tail is partially amputated, the cut
spinal cord is thought to provide neu-
rotrophic factors that promote regen-
eration, and ablation of the spinal
cord5 in adult urodeles completely
prevents blastema formation and sub-
sequent tail regeneration (Holtzer,
1956). In contrast, spinal cord abla-
tion in Xenopus laevis tadpoles re-
duces proliferation and retards differ-
entiation of the notochord cells at the
distal tip, leading to regeneration of a
twisted, shorter tail than controls
(Taniguchi et al., 2008). Early on,
there is no effect of spinal cord abla-
tion on wound healing or muscle
regeneration.
Later,
however,
the
myoﬁbers appear to degenerate, sug-
gesting that the spinal cord provides a
neurotrophic factor needed for sur-
vival of myoﬁbers. One possible rea-
son for the difference in dependence
on the spinal cord for tail regeneration
in urodeles and anurans is that, in
urodeles, cartilage rather than noto-
chord is regenerated after partial tail
removal. In Xenopus, the notochord
provides an additional source of Shh
to the cells near the plane of the am-
putation, so it is conceivable that the
availability of Shh in the stump tis-
sues determines regenerative poten-
tial. In support of this, treatment with
the Shh signaling inhibitor cyclopam-
ine has been shown to prevent tail
regeneration in axolotls (Schnapp et
al., 2005).
The regenerated Xenopus tadpole
tail is not as perfect as it might ﬁrst
5While the regenerating tails have no spinal cord,
peripheral nerves do invade the regenerate (Tan-
iguchi et al., 2008).
XENOPUS REGENERATION 1229

appear (Fig. 1A). The muscle tissue
that is regenerated from the satellite
stem cell population is not clearly seg-
mented (Slack et al., 2008). Spinal
ganglia (dorsal root ganglia), normally
present as organized collections of
neural crest derived sensory neurons
on each somite, are almost entirely
missing, and the normal segmented
pattern of the spinal nerves is not re-
established in the regenerate (Filoni
and Bosco, 1981). Other neural crest
cell derivatives such as melanophores
(pigment cells) are regenerated nor-
mally,
albeit
through
a
different
mechanism (Lin et al., 2007). During
development, the neural crest cells
arise from a region between the neu-
ral plate and ﬂanking epidermis, and
they migrate away after the neural
plate folds up into a tube to form the
spinal cord. This interaction between
epidermal cells and neural plate cells
does not occur during regeneration,
and the replacement melanophores do
not originate from regenerated neural
crest but from existing precursors lo-
cated in the blastema and presumably
recruited from ﬁn tissue near the
stump (Lin et al., 2007).
1.2 Tadpole Hindlimb
Regeneration
Regeneration of the limb buds in Xe-
nopus laevis was ﬁrst described over
50 years ago (Komala, 1957). Regen-
eration of the limb buds is most suc-
cessful during the early stages of limb
differentiation and then declines as
metamorphosis proceeds (Dent, 1962).
The ontogenic decline begins in st. 53
hindlimbs and generally results in a
progressive failure to regenerate toes,
starting with the most anterior toe
(Dent, 1962; Muneoka et al., 1986). A
normal Xenopus hindlimb has ﬁve
toes, and the anterior three toes (dig-
its I, II, and III) have claws on the
distal tip. Dent’s study reports perfect
regeneration at st. 51, failure to re-
cover at least one digit at st. 53, and a
more variable phenotype at st. 55,
with the average number of toes re-
generated being 3.7 at st. 53 and 2.5 at
st. 55. It should be noted that Dent
operated limbs through the distal fe-
mur (stylopod), whereas most contem-
porary studies amputate more dis-
tally, through the knee or ankle joint.
As well as this gradual ontogenic de-
cline, Dent also reports several limbs
that failed to regenerate any struc-
tures (23% at st. 55 and 17% at st. 57).
Development of the skeletal elements
of the hindlimb digits proceeds in a
posterior to anterior order (Alberch
and Gale, 1983), so, the digits that are
last to form are most likely to be ab-
sent from regenerates. At later stages,
the most common outcome after am-
putation was the production of a hy-
pomorphic
regenerate
resembling
those formed after partial amputation
of
postmetamorphic
forelimbs
(see
Section 1.3).
Different laboratories report slightly
different
regenerative
abilities
in
hindlimb buds (see Box 2). It is gener-
ally accepted that limb buds up to st.
52, at which time the ankle constric-
tion is ﬁrst observed (Fig. 3A), are able
to regenerate perfectly. After amputa-
tion the wound is covered quickly by
migrating epithelial cells from the
stump and over the next few days the
multilayered
apical
epithelial
cap
(AEC) is restored (Fig. 3G). The basal
epithelial cells change shape and be-
come columnar (Pearl et al., 2008) and
start expressing AEC markers like
fgf-8 (Christen and Slack, 1997). With
maturation, regeneration ability be-
comes more restricted, resulting in re-
generates that are imperfect, with
missing structures. Therefore, a st. 54
limb, which contains muscle ﬁbers
and cartilage and has a foot with toes
indicated (Fig. 3B), can form a stump
or up to four toes after partial ampu-
tation. A proximal level amputation at
this stage, however, also always leads
to intercalation defects where even
though phalanges are formed, more
proximal elements are missing (Wolfe
et al., 2000; Nye and Cameron, 2005).
Regeneration capacity does not simply
decline in a distal to proximal manner
but also depends on the exact level of
amputation. It has been shown that
regeneration ability is higher if the
amputation plane goes through a joint
region rather than an ossiﬁed region
(Wolfe et al., 2000; Nye and Cameron,
2005). In tadpoles close to metamor-
phosis at st. 57 (Fig. 3C), there is very
little to no regeneration ability left
and amputated limbs either heal the
wound without any visible regenera-
tion or form a small spike. After meta-
morphosis, froglet hindlimbs regain
some capacity of regeneration and
form hypomorphic spikes, containing
mainly cartilage and skin similar to
forelimb regenerates in froglets (Dent,
1962).
Regeneration competent hindlimb
buds consist of mostly undifferenti-
ated mesenchyme, surrounded by epi-
Box 2: Variation in regenerative success of
tadpole hindlimbs
One problem that has come to light in the last few years is the variability
of regeneration success in tadpole hindlimbs. Some labs have seen non-
regenerative limbs at st. 54 while others still report toe regeneration at st.
57 (Slack et al., 2004; Kawakami et al., 2006). This can cause problems when
analysing results from potential gain and loss of function experiments. In
the classical studies, toe regeneration is reported to rapidly fall off between
stages 55 and 57 (Dent 1962, Muneoka 1986) but the number of toes
recovered at earlier stages varies from report to report, possibly due to the
different levels of amputation used (distal femur in Dent, wrist in Muneoka).
Xenopus are not inbred lab strains, and so genetic variation may inﬂuence
regenerative success. To minimise variation in regeneration ability, it has
been suggested that tadpoles used in research should fall within the normal
growth curve for the lab and that any tadpoles taking much longer do
develop will show signiﬁcantly different regeneration ability and add exces-
sively to variation (Nye and Cameron, 2005). These authors also showed
that care should be taken to be consistent in the amputation plane since
small variations in the proximal-distal level of amputation determines
whether the amputation is at the level of a joint or going through a bone
which makes a signiﬁcant difference for regeneration performance (Wolfe et
al., 2000; Nye and Cameron, 2005). We would add to this the need for sibling
and stage matched controls where possible.
1230 BECK ET AL.

dermis. To determine whether the
mesenchyme or the epidermis was re-
sponsible for the decline in regenera-
tion success, Yokoyama et al. created
recombinant limbs, combining mesen-
chyme and epidermis from regenera-
tion competent (st. 52) and incompe-
tent (st. 56) limbs (Yokoyama et al.,
2000). The regenerative mesenchyme
was able to induce fgf-8 expression in
the regenerative incompetent epider-
mis and as a consequence, regenera-
tion could proceed. In contrast, the re-
generative epidermis was unable to
induce nonregenerative mesenchyme
and these limbs failed to regenerate.
These results demonstrate that regen-
eration capacity depends on the mes-
enchyme to supply both signals and
progenitor cells and that nonregen-
erative epidermis retains the capacity
to respond to these stimuli.
The ability of Xenopus to regenerate
limb buds may not seem very remark-
able when compared to the adult limb
regeneration of urodeles such as the
axolotl. However, limb buds do not re-
generate at all in chicks (Summerbell,
1974), and mice can only regenerate
digit tips (Borgens, 1982). The key to
the anuran’s ability to regenerate may
be related to fundamental differences
in the way that limbs develop. The
current model for limb development
has mainly evolved from functional
studies done in chick and mouse and
is generally thought to apply to all

XENOPUS REGENERATION 1231

tetrapod vertebrates. However, there
are several reasons why limb develop-
ment in amphibians might be differ-
ent from that of amniotes. First, there
is a difference in the developmental
timing: in chick and mouse, limb buds
develop during embryogenesis in par-
allel with organogenesis. In contrast,
in Xenopus, limbs develop much later,
after embryogenesis and organogene-
sis is ﬁnished. Second, the origin of
the limb muscle in amphibians is still
unknown. In chick, muscle precursors
from the lateral plate mesoderm mi-
grate into the limb bud (Christ and
Brand-Saberi, 2002), but in Xenopus,
ﬂank muscle in the vicinity of the limb
anlagen is well differentiated by that
time and no migratory muscle precur-
sors have been found (Satoh et al.,
2005b). Third, in chick, the signals for
outgrowth of the limb originate from a
specialized structure at the distal end
of the limb bud, the apical ectodermal
ridge (AER), which extends along the
dorsoventral boundary from anterior
to posterior (Saunders, 1948). Am-
phibians do not have an AER as found
in chick but some anurans, including
Xenopus, have an analogous structure
called the apical epithelial cap (AEC)
that acts to support outgrowth of the
limb bud (Christen and Slack, 1997).
There are also some clear differ-
ences in the expression of key limb
patterning genes between amniotes
and amphibians, suggesting that the
current model for limb development
does not apply to all tetrapod verte-
brates. There is a clear difference in
the initial mechanism of dorsal–ven-
tral
patterning
although
later
re-
stricted expression of lmx1 in the dor-
sal mesoderm appears to be the same
across tetrapod species (Christen and
Slack, 1998; Matsuda et al., 2001).
Genes involved in deﬁning the prox-
imal–distal (P–D) axis such as fgf-10
(Yokoyama et al., 2000) and fgf-8 are
expressed in the distal mesenchyme
and in the apical region of the limb
bud, respectively (Christen and Slack,
1997). However the distal mesenchy-
mal expression of the Bmp inhibitor
Gremlin in mouse limbs, which regu-
lates the growth of the limb by means
of inhibitory effects on the AER spe-
ciﬁc Fgf-8 (Verheyden and Sun, 2008),
is not seen in Xenopus (Pearl et al.,
2008).
Because
regeneration
ability
de-
clines with maturity and there is a
lack of dedifferentiation of mature tis-
sues, such as muscle and cartilage
(from observation of tail regenera-
tion), it has been argued that regener-
ation in Xenopus limbs is not, there-
fore, epimorphic (see Box 3). Limb
stages that are able to regenerate con-
tain undifferentiated cells that would
be able to contribute to the blastema
(see Fig. 3G, proximal limb). There-
fore, it has been suggested that the
lost tissue is replaced by compensa-
tory regulation of normal developmen-
tal mechanisms (Slack et al., 2004).
However, many gene products are ex-
pressed in a different pattern during
regeneration compared with develop-
ment. In a differential screen for
genes involved in lens and st. 51–53
hindlimb regeneration, Wolfe et al.
found half of their 13 selected clones
were highly up-regulated during limb
regeneration but were not expressed
in the early limb bud (Wolfe et al.,
2004; see Section 3.3). Furthermore,
hoxc10 is strongly up-regulated in the
whole regeneration blastema 1 day af-
ter amputation at st. 52 and expres-
sion stays high for at least the next 5
days in the blastema. In contrast,
during limb development, hoxc10 ex-
pression is constant and restricted
to proximal anterior mesenchyme
throughout st. 50–55 (Christen et
al., 2003). Expression of gremlin is
up-regulated in the posterior part of
the blastema mesenchyme during re-
generation (st. 52), but not during
development (Pearl et al., 2008). It
also has been shown that developing
and regenerating limbs react differ-
ently to retinoic acid (Scadding and
Maden, 1986a; see Section 2.2.4).
Thus, there is good evidence that de-
veloping and regenerating limbs are
different and the question of which
cells contribute to the limb blastema
is still open. Unequivocally address-
ing this issue will require lineage
tracing analyses in the limb similar
to those carried out in the regener-
ating tail (Gargioli and Slack, 2004).
1.3 Froglet Forelimb
Regeneration
In contrast to urodele amphibians
such as the axolotl, which is capable of
regenerating a perfect limb as an
adult, Xenopus froglets exhibit a much
more limited regenerative response to
partial amputation of the forelimb,
generating a pattern-deﬁcient struc-
ture known as the hypomorphic spike
(Fig. 4A; reviewed in Suzuki et al.,
2006). These hypomorphic spikes are
equivalent to the structures regener-
ated in postmetamorphic (froglet) Xe-
nopus hindlimbs, originally described
by Dent (1962). Despite the apparent
lack of pattern in the regenerates,
Box 3: Post-metamorphic limb regeneration:
Epimorphosis or tissue regeneration?
A hypomorphic spike is produced when froglet fore or hindlimbs of Xeno-
pus and other anurans are amputated. This contrasts with the situation in
urodeles where a complete and correctly patterned limb is regrown: the
textbook case of epimorphic regeneration (see Box 1 for deﬁnition). Grafting
experiments performed by Goss and Holt in 1992 support the idea that
epithelial-mesenchymal interactions are required for production of the hy-
pomorphic spike (Goss and Holt, 1992). They showed that skinned forelimbs
amputated at wrist level could not form a blastema when inserted into the
abdominal cavity. There is also molecular evidence suggesting that the
forelimb blastema contains intrinsic patterning information (Endo et al.,
2000). Korneluk and Liversage removed the bones of the forearm before
amputation and observed a marked delay in regeneration in Xenopus, but
not in newts. They use this result to argue that regeneration of the froglet
forelimb is a process of tissue regeneration, rather than epimorphosis (Kor-
neluk and Liversage, 1984), a view also expressed by Tassava (Tassava,
2004). However, this interpretation depends on a need for cellular dediffer-
entiation to fulﬁl the deﬁnition of epimorphosis, which was never implied by
Morgan’s original deﬁnition of the term (Morgan, 1901) and is also not
included in more recent deﬁnitions (Carlson, 2007).
1232 BECK ET AL.

similar processes to those described
in urodele limb regeneration occur
initially. A regeneration response is
initiated and proliferation occurs (epi-
morphosis), but subsequently develop-
mental pathways are not re-activated
appropriately to re-establish the axes
of the limb. The froglet forelimb,
therefore, as a “halfway house” regen-
erative event, is a useful system in
which to try and improve regenerative
outcome.
After amputation of the froglet fore-
limb, a wound epidermis forms rap-
idly and covers the cut surface within
24 hr. The wound epidermis has no
accompanying dermis and basement
membrane, and transitions to an or-
ganized, multilayered epithelium, the
AEC. These events lead to the estab-
lishment of a proliferating blastema
(Suzuki et al., 2005), which produces
the hypomorphic spike. While the
spike looks quite well formed from the
outside, the only skeletal structure is
a single, continuous cartilaginous ele-
ment, arising from the cut bone (Fig.
4D). Skin, nerves, and blood vessels
are regenerated normally, but histo-
logical analysis has shown that the
spike contains no muscle, ossiﬁed
bone, tendons, ligaments, or dermis
(Dent, 1962; Korneluk and Liversage,
1984; Satoh et al., 2006). The lack of

XENOPUS REGENERATION 1233

muscle in the spike indicates that the
muscle satellite cells of the stump,
which repopulate the tail in Xenopus,
do not do so in the forelimb. This is not
thought to be due to an unfavorable
blastemal
environment,
because
transplanted muscle satellite cells de-
velop normally into myoﬁbers (Satoh
et al., 2005a). The lack of joints and
ossiﬁcation may be due to failure to
reactivate bone morphogenetic pro-
tein genes such as gdf5 and bmp4 (see
Section 2.2.2).
So what happens to the axes of the
froglet forelimb? Limbs are normally
patterned along three axes during de-
velopment. Lmx-1, a marker of the
dorsal limb, is not re-expressed in fro-
glet forelimb regeneration (Matsuda
et al., 2001). However, male froglets
can regenerate a nuptial pad on the
ventral spike surface (Tassava, 2004)
suggesting dorsal ventral patterning
is re-established through alternative
mechanisms. The nuptial pad consists
of a specialized epidermis character-
ized by regular hook-shaped black
hairs (frog “velcro”) that develops on
the ventral surface of the sexually ma-
ture male forelimb zeugopod and au-
topod and assists the animal in am-
plexus
during
mating
(Deuchar,
1975b). The anterior–posterior axis
depends on expression of shh in the
posterior limb bud early in limb devel-
opment, but again this is not recapit-
ulated during regeneration of the fore-
limb,
where
shh
is
conspicuously
absent (Endo et al., 2000). Indeed, a
superﬁcially similar single spike mor-
phology can be induced by treating re-
generating axolotl limbs with the Shh
inhibitor
cyclopamine
(Roy
and
Gardiner, 2002). However in these
urodeles, bone and joints still form
along the proximodistal axis. Clearly
then, Shh is not the only patterning
molecule deﬁcient in the froglet fore-
limb blastema. There is also molecu-
lar evidence for proximal–distal pat-
terning,
as
hoxA13,
normally
a
marker of the autopod, is expressed in
the distal end of the spike (Endo et al.,
2000).
It is well documented that limb re-
generation in urodeles is nerve depen-
dent (Brockes, 1987). Successful limb
regeneration seems to depend on tro-
phic factors arising from cut axons in
the limb (Brockes, 1984). These fac-
tors are thought to target the wound
epidermis and enable blastema main-
tenance. Nerve dependence has not
been found for Xenopus at a stage
when limbs do regenerate successfully
(Filoni and Paglialunga, 1990; Can-
nata et al., 2001). In contrast, the re-
generation of a hypomorphic spike in
postmetamorphic animals does ap-
pear to be nerve dependent. In Xeno-
pus froglet forelimbs, denervation pre-
vents regeneration, and both blastemal
cell expression of msx1 and wound epi-
dermis expression of fgf8 are known to
be nerve dependent (Endo et al., 2000;
Suzuki et al., 2005).
Like the hindlimb, the froglet fore-
limb regenerates more successfully
when amputated at a more distal level
(Tassava, 2004). Distal amputations
through the radius and ulna (zeugo-
pod) result in 100% spike formation,
with the spike eventually reaching ap-
proximately the same length as the
original limb, and continuing to grow
in homeostasis with the rest of the
body. Amputation through the hu-
merus (stylopod) resulted in 80% re-
generating
spikes,
although
the
spikes
themselves
were
generally
shorter. Body wall level amputations
do not regenerate at all (Tassava,
2004).
1.4 Lens Regeneration
Uniquely
among
the
vertebrates,
some amphibians are able to regener-
ate the lens of the eye after its surgical
removal (lentectomy). The new lens is
formed by transformation of cells from
one differentiated tissue type to an-
other, a process known as transdiffer-
entiation
(reviewed
in
Burke
and
Tosh, 2005). However, the source of
the new lens differs depending on spe-
cies. The best-studied example of lens
regeneration occurs in adult newts,
where the new lens is formed from
pigmented dorsal iris epithelial cells
by a process known as Wolfﬁan regen-
eration (for review see Tsonis, 2006).
In 1963, Freeman described a differ-
ent mechanism in Xenopus laevis tad-
poles, whereby regeneration of the
lens is from cells of the overlying outer
cornea (Freeman, 1963). In lentecto-
mized premetamorphic X. laevis tad-
poles, new lens cells transdifferentiate
from the cells of the central region of
the inner layer of the outer cornea in
response to a factor present in the vit-
reous of the eye (Reeve and Wild,
1981; Filoni et al., 1982). This as yet
unidentiﬁed factor is thought to origi-
nate from the neural retina (Filoni et
al., 1982). This factor, henceforth de-
scribed as the vitreous factor, can
induce lens differentiation in cells
from either the outer cornea or the
surrounding
pericorneal
epidermis,
which together form a region termed
the lentogenic area that covers an
area approximately twice the diame-
ter of the eye (Freeman, 1963). Pieces
of either outer cornea or pericorneal
epidermis from within the lentogenic
area can transdifferentiate into lens
tissue when they are transplanted
into the vitreous chamber of the eye,
where they are exposed to the vitreous
factor (Reeve and Wild, 1978; Bosco et
al., 1980). In this type of assay, which
we suggest could be called an “eye-
steck” graft, the original lens is left in
place, and a new ectopic lens is seen to
develop from the transplant. In con-
trast, epidermal fragments from out-
side the lentogenic area will not form
a lens in this assay and are refractory
to the vitreous factor (Cannata et al.,
2003).
Normally, the outer corneal cells
are protected from contact with the
vitreous factor by means of the physi-
cal barrier formed by the lens itself, as
well as the inner cornea (Fig. 5A). The
ability to replace a lost lens decreases
gradually between st. 50 and 58 and is
lost as the animal progresses through
metamorphosis (Freeman, 1963). The
premetamorphic decline in lens regen-
eration means that, like in the tail and
the limb, it is possible to use both gain
and loss of function approaches to in-
vestigate the mechanisms underlying
lens regeneration. As the tadpole ma-
tures, the inner cornea heals faster,
preventing lens regeneration from oc-
curring (Filoni et al., 1997). If the lens
is replaced by a millipore ﬁlter bar-
rier, corneal transdifferentiation does
not occur either, demonstrating that
mechanical disruption of the contact
between the vitreous factor and the
outer cornea prevents regeneration
(Cioni
et
al.,
1982).
Interestingly,
premetamorphic
Xenopus
tropicalis
tadpoles do not normally regenerate
their lenses due to rapid healing of the
inner cornea in this species, presum-
ably creating such a barrier (Henry
and Elkins, 2001). However, the com-
1234 BECK ET AL.

petence to transdifferentiate X. tropi-
calis cornea to lens can be demon-
strated by transplanting cornea into
the vitreous chamber of the eye, sug-
gesting that both the factor and re-
sponding pathways are conserved be-
tween X. laevis and X. tropicalis. The
competence to respond to this factor is
more restricted than in X. laevis, with
only central corneal cells able to elicit
a regenerative response, while peri-
corneal cells fail (Henry and Elkins,
2001). X. borealis, a closer relative of
X. laevis, similarly appears to have an
active vitreous factor and lens forming
competent
outer
corneal
cells,
as
shown by reciprocal transplantations,
although it does not regenerate a new
lens after lentectomy, probably for the
same reason as X. tropicalis (Filoni et
al., 2006).
Until recently, the loss of regenera-
tive ability at metamorphosis was
thought to be ﬁnal. However, Yoshii et
al. report successful lens regeneration
in juvenile and adult frogs 3–9 months
after metamorphosis, suggesting that,
like limbs, there is some recovery of
regenerative ability after metamor-
phosis is completed (Yoshii et al.,
2007, Box 4). In this case, however,
there does not appear to be any trans-
differentiation of cornea or other eye
tissues, and a possible explanation is
that the lens regenerates from the
lens capsule as it does in mammals
(Gwon et al., 1990).
Several genes encoding transcrip-
tion factors involved in initial develop-
ment of the lens during embryonic
stages (pax6, prox1, otx2, and sox3)
are re-expressed in transdifferentiat-
ing corneal cells, suggesting that re-
generation is at least in part a reca-
pitulation of ontogeny (Schaefer et al.,
1999; Henry et al., 2002). A recent
study showed that the lens regenerat-
ing competence correlates speciﬁcally
to localized pax6 expression in the len-
togenic area, and that misexpression
of pax6 in noncompetent ﬂank epider-
mis enables transdifferentiation into
lens in response to the vitreous factor
(Gargioli et al., 2008). Pax6, then, may
confer on the lentogenic area compe-
tence to respond to the vitreous factor,
re-activating
lens
differentiating
pathways. It would be interesting to
see if pax6 expression is more re-
stricted in X. tropicalis, leading to the
failure of pericorneal cells to respond
to the vitreous factor.
Freeman
described
ﬁve
distinct
stages of cornea-lens transdifferentia-
tion in X. laevis which are still used
today (Freeman, 1963; Fig. 5B–E). In
stage 1, cells of inner layer of the outer
cornea
change
from
squamous
to
cuboidal (1–2 days). By stage 2, small
clusters of these cells are formed by
aggregation (2–3 days). By stage 3,
the aggregate has become a lens vesi-
cle (3–5 days). Stage 4 is deﬁned by
the formation of the primary lens ﬁ-
bers (7–8 days) and stage 5 by forma-
tion
of
the
secondary
lens
ﬁbers
(10–12 days). As Xenopus tadpole de-
velopment varies according to rearing
conditions, these stages should be
used to enable comparison between
results
generated
by
different
re-
searchers. Lentectomy protocols vary
somewhat, but an example is shown in
Figure 5A. After surgical removal of
the lens (lentectomy), the central cells
of the outer cornea, containing the
transit amplifying population, trans-
differentiate into lens, rather than the
peripherally located stem cells. As dis-
cussed
earlier,
these
central
cells
transdifferentiate because they be-
come exposed to an unknown factor
present in the vitreous of the eye, nor-
mally kept separate from the outer
cornea by the neural crest derived in-
ner cornea (Bosco et al., 1979, 1980,
1986; Reeve and Wild, 1981; Filoni et
al.,
1982).
This
factor
appears
to
mimic the lens inducing factors pro-
duced by the optic cup in development
(Cannata et al., 2008). Exposed cor-
neal cells must lose their differenti-
ated state, re-enter the cell cycle and
produce daughter cells of a differently
fated cell type. They must then ar-
range themselves into an organized,
functional lens structure. Three pro-
cesses, therefore, take place sequen-
tially:
de-differentiation
of
corneal
cells, proliferation, and re-differentia-
tion (transdifferentiation) into lens ﬁ-
bers. Of these three processes, only
the ﬁnal two are expected to use mech-
anisms in common with development
of the eye. The earliest stage of regen-
eration is triggered by the unknown
vitreous factor and initiates re-expres-
sion of the developmental genes, such
as pax6, and the functional proteins,
such as the crystallins. As no func-
tional studies have yet been done, it is
not known whether the up-regulation
or activation of one or more of these
eye speciﬁc transcription factors is
sufﬁcient to cause transdifferentia-
tion.
2. MOLECULAR PATHWAYS
INVOLVED IN
REGENERATION
In this section, we will discuss the
ﬁndings of recent functional studies
that implicate certain candidate path-
ways or processes in regeneration. Re-
Box 4: Regenerative decline during metamorphosis
Regeneration experiments involving Xenopus forelimbs almost always use
post-metamorphic froglets, and those involving hindlimbs use pre-metamor-
phic tadpoles. There are two reasons why this is so, ﬁrstly, the tadpole’s
forelimbs develop in a pocket and only break free at st. 58, and secondly,
after metamorphosis and the subsequent loss of the tail, the animals use
their hindlimbs for swimming. This bias conveys the view that forelimbs are
able to regenerate hypomorphic spikes throughout life while hindlimbs
cease to regenerate at around st. 57. In reality, though, both regenerate
hypomorphic spikes post-metamorphosis and hindlimbs often show a refrac-
tory period at around st. 57 when they do not regenerate at all. It has yet to
be demonstrated for forelimbs, but the presence of a refractory period of
failed regeneration during metamorphosis might be more general since
recently it was shown that also lens and retina could regenerate/repair after
metamorphosis contrary to the current paradigm (Yoshii et al., 2007). How-
ever it should be noted that this regeneration of the lens in juvenile frogs
apparently does not involve transdifferentiation from the cornea or other eye
tissues and instead it is thought that the regenerated lens arises from the
remaining lens capsule as in mammals. Dent notes that circulating protein
bound iodine levels rise sharply at st. 57-58 and decline at the time hypo-
morphic spikes are formed (Saxen et al., 1957; Dent, 1962)
XENOPUS REGENERATION 1235

cently, it has also become possible to
chemically
alter
speciﬁc
molecular
pathways by using small molecule in-
hibitors (so-called “chemical genetics,”
Section 2.1 and Table 1). While speci-
ﬁcity can be an issue with this ap-
proach and must be determined for
each new compound, molecules are
usually added directly to the tadpoles’
water, making for a simple assay. The
main disadvantage of a chemical ge-
netic approach is cost, and it is only
really practical when small volumes of
culture medium can be used. While
Xenopus laevis is not considered to be
genetically tractable, there is still
plenty of scope for manipulating gene
expression at late stages (see Section
2.2 and Table 1). Recent studies use a
combination of chemical genetics and
inducible transgenic methodology to
begin to dissect the molecular path-
ways and cellular mechanisms re-
quired for regeneration (Section 2.3).
2.1 Chemical Genetic
Approaches: The Use of
Soluble Inhibitors in
Functional Study of
Regeneration
2.1.1 Tgf- signaling.
Wound healing is the ﬁrst step during
regeneration and it is known that for-
mation of a wound epithelium is an
essential process for successful regen-
eration. Recently, it has been shown
that transforming growth factor-beta
(Tgf-) signaling plays a critical role
in wound healing of the tail (Ho and
Whitman, 2008). Interaction of Tgf-
ligands with two serine-threonine ki-
nase receptors leads to the phosphor-
ylation of these receptors and subse-
quent transduction of the signal to
Smad2 or 3. Upon phosphorylation,
Smad2/3 translocates to the nucleus,
interacts with other transcription fac-
tors, and activates downstream genes.
Several of the Tgf- signaling path-
way components are present in the
right place at the right time to play a
role during tail regeneration. Using a
speciﬁc antibody that recognizes the
activated,
phosphorylated
form
of
Smad2 (pSmad2), Ho and Whitman
showed active Tgf- signaling at the
